game_data.title: Estate of Miriam Vogt v. Trattoria Delvecchio LLC — Wrongful Death & Criminal Negligence Investigation

suspect.name: Marcus Delvecchio
suspect.profile: Marcus Delvecchio grew up in a tight-knit Italian-American household in Newark, the son of a deli worker who preached that feeding people was a kind of sacrament. He moved to the city at twenty-three, washed dishes, bused tables, managed two locations for a mid-tier chain, and at thirty-six opened Trattoria Delvecchio on a shoestring loan. Twelve years later he runs two locations with a loyal local following and a reputation for accessible, honest food. In front of guests he is warm and charismatic; behind the line he is controlling and exacting—staff turnover runs high, but veterans who endure earn fierce loyalty. A suspicious pattern emerges in supplier invoices from the six months preceding a food-poisoning outbreak: food-cost percentages dipped well below industry norms while revenue held steady, consistent with either extraordinary waste reduction or systematic reuse of expired product. His humanizing detail: for ten consecutive Thanksgivings he has closed both restaurants to paying customers and fed anyone who walks through the door, at no charge, funding it entirely himself.

system_config.fear_bar_description: Tracks the cumulative psychological and emotional pressure applied to the suspect during the interrogation session. Higher values indicate the suspect is approaching a crisis threshold — increasing the likelihood of emotional breakdown, partial admission, or defensive lockdown. Values that drop signal the suspect has regained composure and control of the room. The session opens at 20, reflecting Delvecchio's pre-existing anxiety load from active litigation, sleep deprivation, and the weight of the civil action — he enters already primed, not calm.

game_data.context: In early October, twelve patrons who dined at the downtown Trattoria Delvecchio location over a four-day period were hospitalized with confirmed Salmonella Enteritidis infections; Miriam Vogt, age 81, died of septic complications eleven days later. The New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene traced the outbreak to improperly handled chicken piccata and a house-made marsala sauce, both of which tested positive for the pathogen at concentrations consistent with temperature abuse or extended storage beyond safe discard windows. A former line cook, Jorge Medina, filed a whistleblower complaint alleging that Marcus Delvecchio personally directed kitchen staff to relabel refrigerated proteins and reseal prepared sauces with falsified prep dates beginning roughly eight months prior to the outbreak—a period that coincides with the opening of a rival trattoria two blocks away and a measurable compression of Delvecchio's food-cost margins. Delvecchio has denied any such instructions, attributing the outbreak to an isolated temperature failure during a delivery and pointing to his decade-long community record, including annual no-cost Thanksgiving service, as evidence of his commitment to guest welfare. The case is complicated by irregularities in the restaurant's catering revenue records flagged during the health department's financial audit, which Delvecchio's legal team attributes entirely to accounting simplifications and which prosecutors have not yet formally characterized. The Estate of Miriam Vogt is pursuing a wrongful-death civil action while the Manhattan District Attorney's office evaluates whether the evidence supports a criminal negligence indictment.
game_data.verdict_truth_text: Marcus Delvecchio personally instructed at least one kitchen employee — confirmed by Jorge Medina's sworn testimony and corroborated by Delvecchio's own session admission — to relabel refrigerated chicken proteins as safe for additional days of service during a sustained period of financial pressure caused by increased local competition; this instruction directly produced the conditions that allowed Salmonella Enteritidis to reach Miriam Vogt's plate. The extreme polygraph reactivity observed during all financial lines of questioning was generated by a separate and unrelated concealment: a three-year arrangement with brother-in-law Teo Esposito to pass undeclared cash through the restaurant's books as fictitious catering revenue, constituting tax fraud and potential money laundering. Both deceptions were real; only the food-safety wrongdoing is charged in this proceeding, and the financial noise was a deliberate red herring in the physiological record.

dossier.identity_summary: Owner-operator of Trattoria Delvecchio, two licensed locations in continuous operation since 2012. No formal culinary credentials; 25 years of documented food-service industry experience from entry-level to proprietor.

family.name: Sofia Delvecchio (née Esposito)
family.note: Financially and emotionally dependent on restaurant income; visible distress over the civil suit may be used as empathic leverage to soften Delvecchio's composure during interrogation.

family.name: Teo Esposito
family.note: Currently the subject of a separate state financial-crimes inquiry; mention of Esposito's name or restaurant cash-flow records produces disproportionate defensiveness—useful as a wedge topic to destabilize controlled responses.

family.name: Aldo Delvecchio
family.note: Age 76, residing in private assisted-living facility funded entirely out-of-pocket by Marcus; an ongoing financial pressure point that visibly disrupts his affect when raised.

medical.polygraph_effect: Chronic gut-brain axis hyperactivation independently elevates baseline GSR regardless of deceptive intent; GSR spikes alone should not be read as deception indicators—corroborate with respiratory and cardiovascular channels.

Resting blood pressure and baseline heart rate run above population norms; cardiovascular readings will appear elevated across all topics and should be interpreted relative to his individual baseline rather than standard tables.

habits.habit: Daily lisinopril (ACE inhibitor) for hypertension management
habits.polygraph_effect: Minimal suppression of acute sympathetic response; heart-rate readings are largely reliable but resting values are elevated—establish a robust neutral baseline before interpreting reactive spikes.

habits.habit: High caffeine intake: 5 to 6 espresso shots daily, typically consumed before noon
habits.polygraph_effect: Further elevates baseline GSR beyond IBS contribution; combined effect renders GSR an unreliable deception channel for this subject—rely primarily on respiration rate and blood pressure differentials.

habits.habit: Chronic sleep deprivation, self-reported 4 to 5 hours nightly during active litigation
habits.polygraph_effect: Heightened baseline sympathetic arousal increases probability of false-positive reactions across all channels during extended questioning; fatigue-induced micro-pauses before answers may mimic deceptive latency.

priors: 2019: Civil wage-theft complaint filed by two former kitchen employees; matter settled out of court for undisclosed sum, no admission of liability.
2021: New York City Health Department citation at downtown location for improper cold-storage temperature of raw proteins; violation self-corrected and verified on re-inspection within 48 hours.
2023: IRS correspondence audit resulting in $34,000 back-payment for underreported catering revenue; no criminal referral issued at time of resolution.
pressure_points: Aldo's care costs: raising his father's assisted-living expenses visibly destabilizes Delvecchio's composure and can shift him toward justification-framing, making admission-adjacent statements more likely under an EMPATHIC approach.

The fatality: Delvecchio exhibits unscripted, physiologically distinct distress responses when the deceased patron is referenced by name—genuine remorse appears present and can be leveraged to bypass rehearsed deflection.

Teo Esposito or cash-flow irregularities: any probe into restaurant finances or Esposito's name floods deception channels with acute reactivity, potentially masking or amplifying guilt signals on unrelated topics and requiring the operator to carefully sequence financial questions.

node_01_intro.description: Delvecchio enters the interrogation room composed but visibly fatigued — under-eye shadows, a coffee-stained sleeve. He sets both hands flat on the table and offers a measured smile. Establish a solid baseline before committing to a line of attack.

choices.question: Mr. Delvecchio, I want to start by saying I understand this has been an incredibly difficult period — for you, your family, your staff. Before we get into anything formal, can you just tell me, in your own words, what October meant to you as a restaurateur?
choices.answer: He exhales slowly and uncrosses his arms. 'October was the worst month of my life. I built that place with my hands. Every person who walked through that door — I felt responsible for them. And then this happened.' His voice thickens. 'I'm not sleeping. I haven't slept properly in weeks.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: PROGRESS: Empathic opener establishes a lower, usable baseline and builds rapport. Signals are clean here — suspect is being largely genuine. Critical reading window before deception topics are introduced.

choices.question: Mr. Delvecchio, I'd like to walk through your financial records from the eight months prior to the outbreak. Specifically, your food-cost percentages dropped from roughly 32% to under 24% during that window. Can you explain that for me?
choices.answer: His hands tighten slightly under the table. 'Food cost fluctuates. Seasonal menu changes, better supplier terms, tighter ordering — I run a tight ship. There's nothing unusual about that.' He holds eye contact but swallows once before continuing.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WARNING: Financial questions trigger MAX-level reactivity due to the Teo Esposito money-laundering scheme — NOT primarily the food-safety charges. This deception signal is real but traces to an unrelated crime. Players who over-rely on this reading risk misattributing cause. Low positive delta reflects useful but unreliable signal origin.

choices.question: Let's skip the formalities. An 81-year-old woman is dead, twelve people were hospitalized, and your former cook says you personally told staff to relabel rotten food. Why should anyone in this room believe a word you say?
choices.answer: His jaw sets. He glances briefly at the corner of the room where an attorney would sit. 'I'd like to remind you that I'm here voluntarily. If this is how this goes, I want my attorney present for everything else.' He folds his hands and says nothing more.

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Premature aggression without evidence causes suspect to invoke attorney caution and lock down emotionally. Polygraph spikes are fight-or-flight defensive responses, not clean deception reads. Sends interrogation toward fail path immediately.

node_02_food_safety.description: With rapport partially established, the room is quieter. Delvecchio has unclenched somewhat but remains watchful. This is the window to probe specific food-handling practices before introducing witness testimony.

choices.question: In 2021 your downtown location received a Health Department citation for improper cold-storage of raw proteins. At the time you self-corrected within 48 hours. Walk me through what changed in your kitchen protocols after that citation — and whether those changes were still in place this past October.
choices.answer: 'Yeah, we had a walk-in compressor issue. We fixed it. I personally stayed two nights to make sure temperatures were logged every two hours. We were compliant.' A beat. 'October was different. The delivery arrived — the temperature log had a gap on the third.' His eyes drift downward briefly before he catches himself and reestablishes eye contact.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Verbal answer pivots to a prepared delivery-failure narrative, but physiological response elevates specifically on 'October was different.' Eyes-down is a distress-laden tell. Moderate deception signal confirms the scripted deflection. Good progress toward whistleblower confrontation.

choices.question: The DOH pathogen sample came specifically from the house-made marsala sauce and the chicken piccata — both dishes requiring prep the day before service. Who in your kitchen was responsible for those specific items on the days in question, and who signed off on their discard dates?
choices.answer: A pause — longer than it should be for a prepared answer. 'My sous chef, Danny Reyes, runs prep. He signs off on labels.' Then, more carefully: 'I trust my people. I wasn't standing over every container.' He shifts his weight. 'But I — I checked the line. I'm always on the line.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Deflection to the sous chef produces a clear spike cluster — then the suspect's own admission ('I'm always on the line') directly contradicts the deflection in real time. Strong deception pattern. Best path into the whistleblower node with maximum accumulated signal.

choices.question: Your attorney's brief mentions a delivery temperature failure on October 3rd. That seems like a credible explanation — these things happen in commercial kitchens. Is it possible the outbreak was simply an unfortunate supply-chain accident that no one could have predicted?
choices.answer: He visibly relaxes. 'Exactly. That's exactly what happened. These deliveries come in at six in the morning, you're not always there to check every box. It was a tragic accident.' He spreads his hands open. 'I've given my whole adult life to this business. I would never do anything to harm my customers.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Accepting the suspect's prepared narrative hands him full control of the room. Physiological relief indicates he successfully deployed his cover story unchallenged. Insufficient deception evidence accumulated. Leads to degraded outcome.

node_03_financial_pressure.description: Delvecchio's posture tightens noticeably when financial topics enter the room. The catering revenue irregularities flagged by the DOH audit sit on the table between you like a loaded question. Proceed carefully — financial reactivity here may mask more than one secret.

choices.question: Your supplier invoices show protein orders from your primary vendor — Benedetti Foods — dropped by roughly 40% in volume between February and September, while your covers held steady or increased. That gap has to be filled somehow. Where was the protein coming from?
choices.answer: 'We diversified suppliers.' He answers too quickly. 'I was shopping around — prices were up across the board, I was looking for better deals.' A slight tremor in his left hand. 'I have invoices from three other distributors. My attorney can provide those.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: The 'multiple distributors' claim is a prepared deflection — no corroborating invoices were submitted during the DOH audit. Physiological spike is genuine deception tied to the protein-sourcing gap. The tremor is involuntary. Strong evidence-building move toward whistleblower confrontation.

choices.question: I want to understand the catering revenue listed in your books — specifically the accounts tied to the Esposito Group. Can you walk me through what events they were paying for, and who authorized those bookings?
choices.answer: His face drains of color. He looks at the table, then the wall. 'I — the Esposito accounts are... those are family engagements, private events.' He pauses for almost four seconds. 'My attorney has advised me not to discuss internal business accounting in this context.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: MISLEADING SIGNAL: Maximum deception cluster here reflects protection of the Teo Esposito money-laundering scheme — an entirely separate crime unrelated to food safety. Players who interpret this spike as evidence of the outbreak will draw a false inference. Attorney invocation partially locks the financial line. Slightly negative delta because it contaminates subsequent reading environment.

choices.question: Marcus — when a competitor opens two blocks away and starts pulling your regulars, the pressure to cut costs doesn't feel like a choice. It feels like survival. I'm not here to judge that impulse. I'm here to understand what decisions got made and why.
choices.answer: He is quiet for a moment. When he speaks, his voice is lower. 'You have no idea what it's like. Twelve years. I built that place with a sixty-thousand dollar loan and a prayer. And then some VC-backed chain opens down the street and starts undercutting my prices by thirty percent.' He stops. 'I made decisions I... some I'm not proud of.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: PARTIAL TRUTH: 'Decisions I'm not proud of' is a near-admission of significant weight. Physiological profile shows genuine emotional engagement rather than acute suppression — the wall is partially lowering. Strongest progress move from this node; creates the most direct pathway to the Miriam Vogt emotional confrontation.

node_04_whistleblower.description: Jorge Medina worked Delvecchio's line for two years and has provided a sworn statement. Delvecchio knows this testimony exists and has had time to construct a counter-narrative. How you deploy Medina's account will determine whether the suspect fractures or fortifies.

choices.question: I'm going to read you something from Jorge Medina's sworn statement, taken October 18th. Quote: 'Mr. Delvecchio came into the walk-in on a Tuesday evening around 8pm, took a container of pre-cooked chicken off the second shelf, looked at the label, and told me — and I'm quoting exactly — relabel it for Thursday, we're not throwing that out.' End quote. Is Jorge Medina lying?
choices.answer: A long silence. His jaw moves without producing words for almost three seconds. 'Jorge had a grievance against me. I let him go in August.' Another pause. 'I never said those words.' But his breathing has become audible and his right hand closes into a loose fist against the table.

mechanics.gameplay_note: LIE SIGNAL: Maximum deception cluster across all channels. The three-second verbal latency before denial is a critical tell. Fist formation and audible breathing confirm acute stress response to a specific, direct lie. This is the session's strongest isolated deception signal — players should anchor it against the established baseline. Strong path forward.

choices.question: I want you to understand your exposure clearly. Medina's testimony, combined with the DOH pathogen findings and your 2021 citation, builds a pattern-of-negligence argument. The DA's office is evaluating criminal charges — not civil, criminal. Meaning prison. Are you certain you want to stay with the delivery explanation?
choices.answer: He straightens his spine. 'I think we're done for today.' His voice is flat and controlled. 'I came here voluntarily to help resolve this matter and what I'm hearing is a prosecution argument. I'm going to need my attorney before we continue anything further.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Explicit legal threat before extracting an emotional admission triggers full self-protective shutdown. Suspect formally invokes attorney — locking down further productive questioning. Polygraph shows defensive fight-or-flight spike, not a deceptive concealment spike. Signal quality degrades sharply. Forces fail node.

choices.question: Jorge Medina has a four-year-old daughter. He put his livelihood and his reputation on the line to give that statement. He's not a prosecutor. He's a cook who couldn't live with what he knew. What does that tell you?
choices.answer: Delvecchio's expression shifts — something cracks briefly before control returns. 'Jorge was a good cook. I shouldn't have let him go the way I did.' He looks at his hands. 'I'm not going to sit here and call him a liar.' Then, quickly: 'But what he thinks he saw and what actually happened — those can be two different things.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: PARTIAL TRUTH: 'I'm not going to call him a liar' is a significant near-corroboration of Medina's testimony. The verbal walk-back ('two different things') is a defensive maneuver but physiological signals remain continuously elevated — sustained concealment pattern. Strong progress that opens the emotional Miriam Vogt confrontation path.

node_05_miriam_vogt.description: The interrogation has reached its most fragile point. Delvecchio has been pushed toward a partial admission and is showing fatigue cracks. The name Miriam Vogt has not yet been spoken aloud in this room. How you use it now will determine the final outcome.

choices.question: Her name was Miriam. She was 81 years old. She'd been coming to your restaurant for six years — she's in your reservation book eleven times. Her granddaughter said she always ordered the chicken piccata because it reminded her of home. I need you to hear that. Not as a defendant. As the person who built that restaurant.
choices.answer: He doesn't speak for a long moment. His eyes go red at the rims. 'I know who she was.' His voice breaks on the last word. 'I know exactly who she was.' He presses the heel of his hand into his eye. 'I can't...' He steadies himself. 'The chicken. The relabeled chicken. I told Danny it was fine for one more day. I told him that myself.' The admission comes out barely audible.

mechanics.gameplay_note: CONFESSION: Suspect delivers a self-incriminating admission under complete emotional collapse. EEG flatline reflects total override of cognitive suppression — the concealment architecture has failed. This is the session's key deception-to-truth transition. Signals shift from a sustained concealment pattern to raw disclosure. Record this moment against all prior baselines.

choices.question: Let's be direct. You gave the instruction to relabel that chicken. You knew the margins were failing. You knew that sauce was past its date. And you made the call anyway. I need you to say that out loud, right now, in this room.
choices.answer: He absorbs the statement. His face hardens — then unexpectedly does not fully recover. 'You want me to just hand you a conviction.' He breathes once. 'I made decisions under pressure that I...' A long pause. 'I told them the chicken was fine for one more day. I told them that.' His voice is flat and exhausted.

mechanics.gameplay_note: ADMISSION: Direct pressure at maximum emotional fatigue produces a verbal admission — 'I told them the chicken was fine for one more day.' Less complete than the empathic path but constitutes a clear self-incriminating statement. Polygraph shows sustained high deception signal collapsing into disclosure pattern. Strong verdict evidence.

choices.question: You killed her. You killed Miriam Vogt for a few hundred dollars in food-cost savings. You need to accept that and stop wasting everyone's time.
choices.answer: He rises halfway out of his chair, then forces himself back down. When he speaks his voice is low and fully controlled again. 'I am done with this session. You want to charge me, charge me. Until then — nothing. Not another word from me.'

mechanics.gameplay_note: WRONG MOVE: Brutal accusatory framing at the emotional peak triggers a rage response and full shutdown. Signals spike as fight-or-flight — not deception tells. Session ends with no admission and a contaminated polygraph log. Forces fail node.

node_success_confession.description: Delvecchio sits with his elbows on the table and his head partially bowed, no longer managing his affect. The room is quiet except for his uneven breathing. The interrogation has reached its natural end.
node_success_confession.result_text: Delvecchio made at least one self-incriminating statement during this session — specifically acknowledging that he personally instructed kitchen staff that the chicken was safe for one additional day of service. He did not offer a full chronological account and did not address the marsala sauce directly. He has not waived his right to counsel and his attorney will likely move to contextualize or challenge the admission post-session. The polygraph record shows a consistent deception signal across food-safety topics throughout the session, with a late-stage physiological transition from active concealment to disclosure — the clearest evidence pattern available. Note that financial questioning earlier in the session produced a separate MAX-level deception cluster; players should treat those readings as a potentially distinct concealment event before attributing them to the food-safety charges. Render your verdict on the full polygraph log.

node_fail_lockdown.description: Delvecchio has invoked his attorney or emotionally sealed off entirely. His body language is closed, his answers monosyllabic or absent. The room has gone cold. The session is over.
node_fail_lockdown.result_text: The suspect terminated productive engagement before a usable admission could be obtained. Delvecchio shifted to controlled non-cooperation — he offered no further substantive statements and invoked legal caution at least once on the record. Whatever polygraph signal was collected during the completed portion of the session may still carry evidentiary weight: spikes logged during food-safety or kitchen-practice questions should be assessed carefully against the established baseline. Be aware that any extreme financial-topic reactivity observed earlier in the session may reflect a separate concealment concern unrelated to the food-safety charges and should not be weighted as direct evidence of the outbreak. The verdict call is harder from this position — the polygraph log is incomplete and the signal record is ambiguous. Render your verdict on what evidence you have.
